title: TypeScript Generics
summary: Generic functions, interfaces, and classes — writing type-safe code that works across many types without losing specificity.

questions.prompt: What is the key advantage of generics over `any` for a function like `first(arr: any[]): any`?
choices: Generics preserve the type of the input — the return type is inferred from what was passed, not lost as 'any'
questions.explanation: With `any`, TypeScript discards type information — `first([1,2,3])` returns `any` and you can call `.toUpperCase()` on it without error. With `first<T>(arr: T[]): T`, the return is `number` — providing full type checking downstream.

questions.prompt: What type does TypeScript infer for `result`?
questions.explanation: TypeScript infers type arguments from the call: `A = string` from `'hello'`, `B = number` from `42`. The return type is `[string, number]` — a tuple, not a union array.

questions.prompt: The constraint `<T extends { length: number }>` means:
choices: T can be any type that has a numeric `length` property — strings, arrays, custom objects
T must extend the Number class
questions.explanation: The constraint requires a structural minimum — any type with a `length: number` property satisfies it. That includes strings, arrays, and any object with that field. This is structural typing applied to generic constraints.

questions.prompt: What happens?
choices: TypeScript compile error — 'email' is not a key of the user type
questions.explanation: `K extends keyof T` constrains the key argument to only the actual keys of the object. `'email'` is not a key of `{ name: string; age: number }`, so TypeScript rejects it at compile time — protection the `any` approach cannot provide.

questions.prompt: What is `keyof User` when `User = { name: string; age: number; email: string }`?
choices: { name: string; age: number; email: string }
questions.explanation: `keyof T` produces a union of the literal types of all property names in T. For `User`, that's `'name' | 'age' | 'email'`. This is the foundation of type-safe property lookup.

questions.prompt: What is the type of `nameType`?
questions.code:
interface User { name: string; age: number; }
type nameType = User['name'];
questions.explanation: Indexed access type `T[K]` looks up the type of property K in type T. `User['name']` = `string`. This is the type-level equivalent of `user.name` at runtime.

questions.prompt: In a `.tsx` file, why does `<T>` before an arrow function cause a parse error?
choices: TypeScript doesn't support generics in .tsx files
The parser interprets `<T>` as the start of a JSX element, not a generic type parameter
tsx requires class components for generics
questions.explanation: In `.tsx` files, `<T>` is ambiguous — is it JSX or a generic? TypeScript/Babel parsers treat it as JSX. The fix: add a trailing comma `<T,>` or use `function` syntax — the comma signals it's a type parameter list.

questions.prompt: Which are valid uses of generic type constraints?
choices: <T extends string>  — T must be string or a string literal type
<T extends object>  — T must be a non-primitive type
<K extends keyof T> — K must be one of T's property names
<T extends any>     — identical to unconstrained T
questions.explanation: All four are syntactically valid. However `<T extends any>` is redundant — it doesn't add any constraint. `<T extends string>` means T can be `string`, `'hello'`, or any string literal. All four compile and are used in real TypeScript code.

questions.prompt: A generic class `Stack<T>` is instantiated as `new Stack<number>()`. What happens if you call `stack.push('hello')`?
choices: Compiles and works — Stack accepts any type
TypeScript compile error — 'hello' is a string, but T was set to number
questions.explanation: Once `T` is bound to `number` at instantiation, all uses of `T` in the class become `number`. Calling `push('hello')` violates the `push(item: T): void` signature — TypeScript rejects it at compile time.

questions.prompt: What does TypeScript infer for the type of `val`?
questions.explanation: `box.value` is `string[]` (T = string[]). `box.value[0]` indexes into a string array, giving `string`. TypeScript tracks through the generic parameterisation all the way to the element type.

questions.prompt: Default type parameters `<T, E = Error>` mean:
choices: E is optional — if not provided, TypeScript uses Error; if provided, uses the given type
T defaults to Error if not provided
Default type params are only for functions, not interfaces
questions.explanation: Default type params work like function default arguments. `Result<string>` uses `E = Error`. `Result<string, string>` uses `E = string`. Reduces boilerplate when the second param is rarely changed.

questions.prompt: Which pattern ensures type-safe object cloning for any object type?
choices: function clone<T extends object>(obj: T): T { return { ...obj } as T; }
function clone<T>(obj: T): object { return { ...obj } as object; }
questions.explanation: `<T extends object>` constrains to non-primitives and preserves the exact type. The caller gets the same type back, not the widened `object`. Option A loses type. Option C returns widened `object`. Option D loses the return type.

questions.prompt: TypeScript generics are erased at runtime. Which implications follow from this?
choices: You cannot use instanceof to check a generic type T at runtime
Generic type parameters have zero performance cost at runtime
TypeScript can enforce T constraints at runtime automatically
A `Map<string, User>` at runtime is identical to a `Map<string, Product>`
questions.explanation: Generic types are compile-time only. At runtime, all generics are erased — `Map<string, User>` is just `Map`. `instanceof T` is impossible. No runtime cost. TypeScript cannot auto-enforce generic constraints at runtime — you must write guards yourself.

questions.prompt: What type does TypeScript assign to `out`?
questions.code:
function identity<T>(x: T): T { return x; }
const out = identity({ name: 'Alice', age: 30 });
questions.explanation: TypeScript infers T from the argument — T becomes `{ name: string; age: number }`. The return type is T, so `out` is fully typed with the exact shape. No information is lost.

questions.prompt: What does `T[K]` represent in TypeScript's type system?
choices: Indexed access type — the type of property K in type T
questions.explanation: `T[K]` is an indexed access type — it looks up the type of property named by the key type `K` in the type `T`. This is the type-level equivalent of `obj[key]` at runtime.

questions.prompt: A generic function infers type arguments. When would you provide explicit type arguments?
choices: When TypeScript cannot infer from the arguments (e.g., no arguments to infer from) or when you want to override the inferred type
questions.explanation: Explicit type args: `identity<string[]>([])` — an empty array `[]` would otherwise be inferred as `never[]`. When there's no argument to infer from, an explicit argument is needed. Also useful to be explicit for documentation clarity.

questions.prompt: Will this compile? If yes, what is the type of `result`?
choices: Compiles; result is { x: number } & { y: string } which equals { x: number; y: string }
Error — cannot use & in return type
questions.explanation: Both args satisfy `extends object`. TypeScript infers T = `{ x: number }`, U = `{ y: string }`. The return is `T & U` = `{ x: number; y: string }`. `result.x` is `number` and `result.y` is `string` — both accessible with full type safety.

questions.prompt: Why does `<T extends string>` not mean 'T is always exactly string'?
choices: It does — T is always string
T extends string means T is string or any string literal subtype like 'hello' or 'north' | 'south'
extends in generics means the same as extends in class inheritance
questions.explanation: `extends` in constraints means 'is assignable to'. Since `'hello'` is assignable to `string`, T could be `'hello'`. Since `'north' | 'south'` is assignable to string, that's valid too. It sets a lower bound, not an exact type.

questions.prompt: The pattern `<K extends keyof T>` is particularly useful for:
choices: Writing functions that accept a property name and return the correct value type for that property
questions.explanation: `getProperty<T, K extends keyof T>(obj: T, key: K): T[K]` is the canonical example. The return type `T[K]` changes based on which key you pass — if you pass `'name'` you get `string`, if `'age'` you get `number`. Static safety for dynamic key access.

questions.prompt: Which TypeScript built-in utilities are generic types?
questions.explanation: `typeof` is a type operator, not a generic type. `Partial<T>`, `Promise<T>`, and `Array<T>` are all generic types — they accept a type parameter that determines their behaviour and the types of their values.

flashcards.back: Generics preserve type information through transformations. `any` discards it. `first<T>(arr: T[]): T` returns the same type as input. `first(arr: any[]): any` loses it.
`<T extends X>` — T must be assignable to X. Use for: `extends object`, `extends string`, `extends { length: number }`, `K extends keyof T`.
Produces a union of T's property name literals. `keyof { name: string; age: number }` = `'name' | 'age'`. Foundation of type-safe dynamic property access.
Looks up the type of property K in type T. `User['name']` = `string`. With generic K: the return type changes per key passed — no casting needed.

flashcards.front: Generic inference — when to be explicit
flashcards.back: TypeScript infers from arguments. Be explicit `<string[]>` when args are empty (`[]` → `never[]`) or when you want to constrain the inferred type to a wider type.
`<T>` in `.tsx` is parsed as JSX. Fix: trailing comma `<T,>` or use `function` syntax. Only affects `.tsx` files.
Generics are fully erased. `Stack<number>` at runtime is just `Stack`. You cannot `instanceof T`, check T's name, or enforce T at runtime. Guards must be written manually.
`<T, E = Error>` — E is optional; defaults to Error. Allows `Result<string>` while still supporting `Result<string, string>`. Like function default args, at the type level.

project.brief: Design a type-safe `Repository<T>` generic class for a data layer. It manages a collection of entities where each entity has an `id: string`. Requirements: 1) The class parameter T must be constrained to have `id: string`. 2) Methods: `add(entity: T): void`, `getById(id: string): T | undefined`, `getAll(): T[]`, `update(id: string, changes: Partial<T>): T | undefined`. 3) Write out the generic class signature and all method signatures. 4) Explain why `Partial<T>` is the right type for `changes` in `update`. 5) A colleague suggests using `getById(id: string): any` — explain what type safety is lost and how the generic return type fixes it.

checklist.text: Repository<T extends { id: string }> constraint declared correctly
All four method signatures typed without any casts or any
Partial<T> for update.changes explained — makes all fields optional so partial updates work
Explains loss from any return: callers get any, .name/.email access not type-checked
Explains generic benefit: callers of getById<User>() get User | undefined with full type checking

hints: Constraint: `class Repository<T extends { id: string }>` — T must have the id field.
update returns T | undefined — undefined if the id doesn't exist. Partial<T> from utility types.
With any return: `repo.getById('1').nme` compiles (typo) and crashes at runtime.
With T return: `repo.getById('1')?.nme` — TypeScript flags the typo. No cast needed.
